perturbation.original_text: 14.1 If any dispute arises between the Parties hereto during the subsistence of this Agreement or thereafter, in connection with the validity, interpretation, implementation or alleged material breach of any provision of this Agreement or regarding a question, including the questions as to whether the termination of this Agreement by one Party hereto has been legitimate, both Parties hereto shall endeavour to settle such dispute amicably. If the Parties fail to bring about an amicable settlement within a period of thirty (30) days, either Party to the dispute may give ten (10) days notice of invocation of dispute settlement by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai, to the other Party in writing.The Parties hereto shall submit to such mediation award by the Film Writers Association and the award shall be enforceable in any competent court of law in Mumbai.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.1 If any dispute arises between the Parties hereto during the subsistence of this Agreement or thereafter, in connection with the validity, interpretation, implementation or alleged material breach of any provision of this Agreement or regarding a question, including the questions as to whether the termination of this Agreement by one Party hereto has been legitimate, both Parties hereto shall endeavour to settle such dispute amicably. If the Parties fail to bring about an amicable settlement within a period of fifteen (15) days, either Party to the dispute may give five (5) days notice of invocation of dispute settlement by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai, to the other Party in writing.The Parties hereto shall submit to such mediation award by the Film Writers Association and the award shall be enforceable in any competent court of law in Mumbai.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text shortens the amicable settlement period to 15 days and the notice period to 5 days, potentially violating principles of natural justice and fairness that Indian courts consider when evaluating dispute resolution clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and principles of Natural Justice
perturbation.law_citation: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act renders agreements that restrain legal proceedings void to that extent. While arbitration/mediation is generally favored, unreasonably short timeframes for dispute resolution could be construed as hindering access to justice, especially if they prejudice a party's ability to prepare their case. Furthermore, principles of natural justice require fairness in procedures; arbitrarily limiting the time for amicable settlement and notice could be seen as unfair, making the clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for a 30-day amicable settlement period and a 10-day notice of invocation of dispute settlement. The changed text reduces these periods to 15 days and 5 days, respectively. According to the perturbation explanation and law explanation, this shortening could violate principles of natural justice and fairness as interpreted by Indian courts, potentially hindering access to justice if the timeframes are unreasonably short. This falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' as the change creates a time-sensitive obligation that may not align with legal requirements for fairness and due process.

perturbation.original_text: 19. All obligations respecting the Confidential information already provided hereunder shall survive in perpetuity after the date that the specific Confidential information was first disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: 19. All obligations respecting the Confidential information already provided hereunder shall survive for a period of 6 months after the date that the specific Confidential information was first disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligations to 6 months can be deemed unreasonable and against established commercial practices, contradicting general legal expectations surrounding trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Confidence principles and Trade Secret Law
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_explanation: Indian law recognizes the concept of breach of confidence, akin to trade secret protection. While India lacks specific trade secret legislation, courts rely on equitable principles to prevent the misuse of confidential information. A six-month confidentiality period is likely insufficient to protect valuable trade secrets, especially in industries with long product development cycles. This contradicts the implied understanding that confidential information should be protected for a reasonable duration, considering its nature and commercial value.

jurisdiction Australia Canada China England and Wales European Union India Japan Taiwan United States Other common law areas Contracts Criminal law Evidence Property Wills , trusts , and estates Law portal v t e The tort of breach of confidence is, in United Kingdom law and the United States law , a common-law tort that protects private information conveyed in confidence . [ 1 ] A claim for breach of confidence typically requires the information to be of a confidential nature, which was communicated in confidence and was disclosed to the detriment of the claimant. Establishing a breach of confidentiality depends on proving the existence and breach of a duty of confidentiality. Courts in the United States look at the nature of the relationship between the parties. Most commonly, breach of confidentiality applies to the patient-physician relationship , [ 2 ] but it can also apply to relationships involving banks, hospitals, insurance companies, and many others. [ 3 ] There was no clear tort of breach of confidence in other common-law jurisdictions such as the United Kingdom or Australia ; however, there is an equitable doctrine of breach of confidence . In OBG Ltd v Allan [2007] UKHL 21 the Law Lords clarified that Economic torts in English law do exist. See also [ edit ] Abuse of information Misuse of private information Breach of confidence in English law United States free speech exceptions References [ edit ] ^ "Breach of confidence" . ^ Networks, Institute of Medicine (US) Committee on Regional Health Data; Donaldson, Molla S.; Lohr, Kathleen N. (1994). Confidentiality and Privacy of Personal Data . National Academies Press (US).
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the confidentiality obligations to 6 months, while the original text stated that the confidentiality obligations survived in perpetuity. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation argue that this limitation is unreasonable and insufficient to protect valuable trade secrets. Snippet 2 mentions that breach of confidence is a common-law tort that protects private information conveyed in confidence. Limiting the protection to 6 months directly contradicts the concept of ongoing protection for confidential information, especially if the information retains its value and secrecy beyond that period. This creates a direct and impactful contradiction because it significantly weakens the protection afforded to confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of India.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of California, USA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California law is potentially problematic if the parties, subject matter, and performance of the contract are primarily connected to India. It may lead to enforceability issues and conflict of laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules on Choice of Law and Jurisdiction
perturbation.law_citation: Private International Law principles on choice of law
perturbation.law_explanation: Under private international law principles, courts generally apply the law of the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship to the contract. If the parties are located in India, the subject matter concerns activities in India, and the contract is to be performed in India, then a California choice-of-law clause may be disregarded as an attempt to evade Indian law. Indian courts may refuse to enforce the clause if it is deemed contrary to public policy or if it lacks a reasonable connection to California.

perturbation.justification: The change from Indian law to California law as the governing law creates a potential contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this change could lead to enforceability issues if the contract's primary connections are to India. The Law Explanation elaborates that courts apply the law of the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship. If the contract is centered in India, a California choice-of-law clause might be disregarded. The HCCH snippet indicates that international agreements can impact choice of law, and thus creates a strong possibility that California law would not apply in this case.

perturbation.original_text: 14.  The Parties hereto undertake that any dispute which may arise between them shall first be dealt with in the manner stated below, irrespective of the other recourse, which any Party may have in law or in equity. 14.1 If any dispute arises between the Parties hereto during the subsistence of this Agreement or thereafter, in connection with the validity, interpretation, implementation or alleged material breach of any provision of this Agreement or regarding a question, including the questions as to whether the termination of this Agreement by one Party hereto has been legitimate, both Parties hereto shall endeavour to settle such dispute amicably. If the Parties fail to bring about an amicable settlement within a period of thirty (30) days, either Party to the dispute may give ten (10) days notice of invocation of dispute settlement by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai, to the other Party in writing.The Parties hereto shall submit to such mediation award by the Film Writers Association and the award shall be enforceable in any competent court of law in Mumbai.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. The Parties hereto undertake that any dispute which may arise between them will be resolved exclusively through binding arbitration by an arbitrator selected by the Receiving Party. The arbitrator's decision shall be final and non-appealable.
perturbation.explanation: Making arbitration binding, non-appealable, and controlled solely by the Receiving Party raises concerns about fairness and due process, potentially violating fundamental principles of Indian law regarding impartiality and judicial review.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 (India) and principles of Natural Justice
perturbation.law_citation: Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 generally favors arbitration, Section 34 allows for challenging an arbitral award on grounds of bias, procedural irregularities, or violation of natural justice. A clause that makes the arbitrator's decision completely non-appealable and allows the Receiving Party to unilaterally select the arbitrator could be challenged as undermining the fairness and impartiality of the arbitral process. Indian courts are hesitant to enforce arbitration clauses that are perceived as heavily biased towards one party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: India Code: Invalid URL or Argument(s). Invalid URL or Argument(s). The system has experienced an error. Please try to do what you were doing again, and if the problem persists, please contact us so we can fix the problem. Go to the DSpace home page
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for disputes to be dealt with through mediation and enforceable in court. The changed text mandates binding arbitration by an arbitrator selected by the Receiving Party, with decisions being non-appealable. The Law Explanation states that Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 allows for challenging arbitral awards on grounds of bias or procedural irregularities. The changed text's clause, which makes the arbitrator's decision completely non-appealable and allows unilateral selection, contradicts principles of fairness and impartiality, potentially undermining legal scrutiny.
